methods.description: Process for analyzing foraminifera in shale and mudstone samples to understand paleoenvironmental conditions.

steps.description: We have used around 20 g of sample (shales and mudstones) for processing. The sample was treated with H2O2 initially for removal of any organic material.
steps.searchable_summary: Treat samples with H2O2 to remove organic material

steps.description: The residue left was then mixed with water and heated after adding Na2CO3 to it for half an hour.
steps.searchable_summary: Mix residue with water and Na2CO3, then heat

steps.description: The residue was washed over a > 63 µm sieve.
steps.searchable_summary: Wash residue over a > 63 µm sieve

steps.description: The washed residue was dried at 60°C temperature in an oven.
From each representative sample obtained by using a microsplitter, a minimum of 300 foraminifera were picked and represented as the 'total foraminifera number'.
steps.searchable_summary: Pick and count a minimum of 300 foraminifera from each sample

steps.description: We have used the TILIA 2.0.2 software by Grimm (2004) to plot the percentage distribution data of each species.
steps.searchable_summary: Plot percentage distribution data using TILIA 2.0.2

steps.description: The identification of foraminifera was carried out by following the works of Murray and Wright (1974), Singh and Kalia (1970), Kalia (1978), Loeblich Jr. and Tappan (2015) and Pearson et al. (2005).
The paleoenvironment and paleodepositional analysis has been carried out by using foraminiferal morphogroups.
Using their technique to record the foraminiferal morphogroups, we have divided the smaller benthic foraminifera further into the rectilinear benthic foraminifer (RBF) morphogroup.
steps.searchable_summary: Classify benthic foraminifera into RBF morphogroup
